text: The surgical team utilizes four robotic ports, with the camera positioned in the supraumbilical space. The left-handed instrument is placed in the right mid quadrant, and two instruments are in the left mid and left upper quadrants. A 5mm trocar in the right upper quadrant is used for the liver retractor. The initial laparoscopic view shows no gross abnormalities. The team performs lysis of adhesions to create space distal to the vein of Mayo, an anatomical landmark of the pylorus, and begins a five-centimeter myotomy starting two and a half centimeters proximal to the vein of Mayo.

The surgeon marks the area with cautery two and a half centimeters distal to the vein of Mayo at the level of the pylorus. Two anchoring sutures are placed to delineate the vein and facilitate manipulation of the bowel, aiding in positioning for gastrointestinal tract activity.

The surgeon performs a gastroduodenotomy using scissors and cautery, with Dr. Lee opening the gastric lumen using force bipolar. Care is taken to avoid injury to the back wall of the pylorus. The myotomy is extended distally by performing a duodenotomy, revealing the change in wall thickness from the pylorus to the duodenum. A 5 cm gastroduodenostomy is completed, followed by pyloroplasty using a 3-0 V-lock absorbable suture. The superior edge is delineated by an anchoring stitch, and the pylorus is elevated and retracted using the fourth arm. Closure is done transversely, taking small bites of mucosa and large bites of serosa, with each suture cinched individually. A 30-degree angle scope is used for better visualization of the operative site.

The surgeon addresses a dog ear at the inferior aspect of the pyloroplasty by instructing Dr. Lee to dunk the edge, effectively obliterating it. Dr. Lee uses the fourth arm for adequate exposure and tissue retraction. The team decides to over-sew the pyloroplasty with interrupted thoracovacral sutures.
